title: SEGMENT 8 — Integration-free reprogramming: getting the virus out of the picture

lines.text: SEGMENT 8 — Integration-free reprogramming: getting the virus out of the picture
The fundamental problem is this: you need to express OSKM transiently inside a cell for a few weeks, long enough to flip it into pluripotency.
You don't need those factors there forever — once the cell has reset into the pluripotent state, its own endogenous Oct4, Sox2, and so on come on and maintain pluripotency for free.
You only need the exogenous factors as a kick-start.
Once the engine catches, you can turn off the starter motor.
The original retroviral approach was a hammer.
It permanently integrated the four factor genes into the cell's chromosomes.
Permanent expression.
Permanent risk of insertional mutagenesis.
Permanent presence of integrated viral DNA in any cell you'd later try to use clinically.
We needed something gentler.
The first attempt was episomal plasmids.
Plasmids are pieces of circular DNA — you remember from the plasmids lecture — that can exist inside a cell without integrating into the chromosomes.
Researchers built plasmids carrying the four reprogramming factors and transfected them into fibroblasts.
The plasmids floated in the nucleus, the genes were expressed, the cell got reprogrammed, and then over time, as the iPSCs divided, the plasmids got diluted out and disappeared.
The final iPSC contained no transgene.
Clean.
Safe.
The downside: efficiency was low, even lower than retroviral reprogramming.
But it worked.
Then came Sendai virus.
Sendai is an RNA virus that replicates entirely in the cytoplasm of the cell — it never goes into the nucleus, never integrates into chromosomes.
You can package the four reprogramming factors into a modified Sendai virus, infect fibroblasts with it, and the virus will produce the OSKM proteins from cytoplasmic RNA.
The cell gets reprogrammed.
Then, since Sendai is an RNA virus that doesn't integrate, the viral RNA gets degraded and diluted out over cell divisions, and a few weeks later the iPSC is completely free of any viral material.
Sendai reprogramming is now one of the most widely used commercial approaches — kits are sold by major suppliers, and most academic labs that make iPSCs use Sendai.
The analogy is: deliver the factors with a virus that doesn't integrate, so the iPSC doesn't have any virus DNA left behind.
Other approaches followed.
Synthetic mRNA reprogramming, where you literally make mRNA molecules encoding the four factors and deliver them directly into the cell, day after day, for a few weeks.
The mRNA gets translated into protein, does its work, and then degrades within hours — there's no DNA involved at all, no possibility of integration.
The downside is you have to redose the cells repeatedly, because mRNA is short-lived.
The upside is the iPSC is completely synthetic-material-free.
Protein delivery — where you produce purified OSKM proteins and somehow get them into the cells, either using cell-penetrating peptide tags or special delivery techniques.
This works in principle but is technically harder and has been less widely adopted.
microRNA reprogramming — where short RNA molecules that regulate gene expression are used to shift the cell toward pluripotency, sometimes alongside reduced numbers of the OSKM factors.
And then, in some of the most striking recent work, chemical reprogramming — where small-molecule drugs alone, with no transcription factors at all, can drive cells through pluripotency.

The chemical cocktails were developed over a decade of careful screening, mostly out of labs in China, and they showed that the OSKM logic could be replaced entirely by a recipe of small molecules that hit the same chromatin and signaling targets.

That work is, in some ways, even more remarkable than Yamanaka's original — it suggests that pluripotency is not even fundamentally about transcription factor identity; it's about the chromatin state those factors create, and you can recreate that state in other ways.
Today, in 2026, the standard practice in a well-run iPSC lab is to use Sendai virus or episomal plasmids for reprogramming.
The resulting iPSCs are integration-free, with no permanent transgenes, no viral DNA in the genome.
They are, in principle, safe enough to imagine using in patients.
The c-Myc cancer worry that hung over the field in 2007 has, by careful engineering of delivery methods, been substantially defused.
